summary:
The An Lushan Rebellion (755–763 AD) was one of the most devastating civil wars in Chinese history and the turning point that ended the [[event:2152|Tang Dynasty's golden age]] — a rebellion led by the Tang general An Lushan (of Sogdian-Turkic origin) that captured both Chang'an and Luoyang, forced Emperor Xuanzong to flee, and triggered a catastrophic population collapse estimated at 36 million dead (from fighting, famine, and plague) that reduced China's population from approximately 53 million to 17 million. This represents one of the largest demographic catastrophes in human history before the 14th century.

An Lushan had risen from humble origins through military ability to command three of the Tang's nine frontier armies — an accumulation of military power the Tang court's factional politics allowed him to achieve. His rebellion began in December 755 AD and the Tang armies proved incapable of stopping his advance; Emperor Xuanzong fled Chang'an, and his concubine Yang Guifei (the great romance of Chinese history, celebrated in Bai Juyi's 'Song of Everlasting Regret') was executed by mutinous soldiers who blamed her family's influence for the catastrophe. The rebellion was eventually suppressed with the help of Uyghur allies and Turkish mercenaries, but at enormous cost: the Tang was forced to grant autonomy to regional military governors (jiedushi), permanently decentralizing power and weakening the dynasty. The poet Du Fu's greatest works were written during this period, recording the suffering of ordinary people with compassion that made him China's moral poet par excellence.
